message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 37
Recent Green phase: Added per-type damage-count validation. New helper damageCountByType(damages) builds a Record<string,number> keyed by damage.itemType. validateDamages now has a third rejectFirstInvalid check: for each damage, damageCountByType(incident.damages)[itemType] must be <= insuredCounts[itemType] (where insuredCounts = countByType(policy.items)), else throws `More damages than insured items of type: ${itemType}`.

Note a minor inefficiency: damageCountByType(incident.damages) is recomputed inside the predicate for each damage. Consider hoisting it out of the predicate (compute once). Also note countByType (counts Item[] by item.type) and damageCountByType (counts Damage[] by damage.itemType) are structurally similar — consider whether a shared generic count-by-key helper reduces duplication without raising complexity.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run `pnpm test` to verify.

IMPORTANT constraints:
- Do NOT change observable behavior. Keep validation order (negative-amount, then item-in-policy, then count) and exact error messages.
- Do NOT rewrite premium `base + base * rate` forms.
- Preserve premium Math.ceil, payout Math.floor, processClaim's remainingCap mutation.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
37 tests passing, ESLint clean. Now the PRE measurements for the candidate functions.

## PRE-Measurement

**Step 0 — ESLint:** 0 smells across the whole file. Cognitive complexity: all functions below the SonarJS threshold (no violations reported).

**Step 1 — Naming Evaluation:**
- `damageCountByType(damages)` — counts Damage[] by `itemType`. Clear, but structurally identical to `countByType(items)` which counts Item[] by `type`. Two names for the same operation keyed on different fields.
- `countByType` — clear.

**Step 2 — APP Mass (target: `validateDamages`, the function the Green note flags):**

The third `rejectFirstInvalid` recomputes `damageCountByType(incident.damages)` inside the predicate, which runs once per damage. The Green note explicitly calls this out as inefficiency.

```
validateDamages — current:
- Bindings: incident, policy, insuredCounts, 3× damage params, damage.amount(x2), itemType refs... 
- Invocations: rejectFirstInvalid(×3)=6, insuredItemFor=2, countByType=2, damageCountByType=2 (inside predicate) → 12
- Conditionals: 0 explicit (the <= comparison is in arrow) 
- Assignments: 1 (insuredCounts binding via const = not mutation)
Approx mass dominated by 12 invocations = 24+ ; key issue: damageCountByType invocation lives inside the per-damage predicate.
```

**Step 3 — McCabe (`validateDamages`):** Start 1; the predicates contain comparisons but no branching statements in the function body itself. McCabe = 1. The complexity here is duplication and a recomputation inefficiency, not branching.

**Decision:** No McCabe hotspot. The strongest measured lever is **Rule 3 (No Duplication)** + the flagged recompute inefficiency:
1. Hoist `damageCountByType(incident.damages)` out of the predicate (compute once).
2. Unify `countByType` and `damageCountByType` into one generic count-by-key helper to remove conceptual duplication.

Let me apply change #1 first (hoist), run tests, then change #2.

  countByKey(damages, (damage) => damage.itemType);

content.text:
All 37 tests pass, ESLint clean.

## POST-Measurement

**Step 5 — ESLint:** 0 smells → 0 smells (no new violations introduced).

**Step 6 — APP + McCabe deltas:**

`validateDamages` (hoist change):
- The `damageCountByType` invocation moved from inside the per-damage predicate (executed N times) to a single hoisted `const damageCounts` binding. Invocation cost in the predicate: was 1 call-per-element, now 0; one new top-level invocation.
- APP mass of the predicate dropped by removing 1 invocation (−2 mass) and 1 array index from the inner expression; net mass slightly lower and the hot-path call count is now constant rather than O(N).
- McCabe: 1 → 1 (unchanged — no branches involved).

Duplication (generic helper):
- Two near-identical 6-line count loops (`countByType`, `damageCountByType`) → one generic `countByKey<T>` plus two trivial one-line adapters.
- The duplicated `for`-loop body (loop mass 5 + assignment 6 + invocation) existed twice; now exists once. Net APP mass across the file dropped by roughly one full loop+assignment block (~12 mass) since the second copy is gone.
- `countByKey` McCabe: 1 (one loop, no branching). Each adapter McCabe: 1.

**Naming:** `countByKey` reveals the generalized intent; `countByType` and `damageCountByType` retained as intent-revealing domain-specific adapters (Rule 2 preserved over collapsing everything to raw `countByKey` calls at use sites).

**Behavior preservation:** Validation order unchanged (negative-amount → item-in-policy → count). Error messages byte-identical. No premium/payout/cap logic touched.

```
🔄 Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: (1) Hoisted damageCountByType out of the per-damage predicate (compute once);
             (2) Extracted generic countByKey<T> helper; countByType and damageCountByType
                 now delegate to it, removing duplicated count loops.
Smell delta:            0 → 0
Cognitive delta:        below threshold → below threshold (no change in branching)
APP mass delta:         validateDamages predicate −2 (removed per-element invocation);
                        file-level −~12 (one duplicated count loop eliminated)
McCabe delta:           validateDamages 1 → 1; countByKey 1; adapters 1 (no branching anywhere)
Tests:                  37/37 passing ✅
```

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_03-48-11_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Both flagged issues from the Green phase note are resolved: the recompute-in-predicate inefficiency (hoisted) and the structural duplication between `countByType`/`damageCountByType` (unified via `countByKey`), without raising complexity or changing observable behavior.
